projects.title: Guest Pass
projects.problem: Guest Pass is a feature I designed for an all-in-one access control security system called Elements during my time at LenelS2. It allows users to send a temporary “guest pass” that visiting guests can access right from their smart phone.

caseStudy.title: Overview
paragraphs: Guest Pass is a feature I designed for an all-in-one access control security system called Elements during my time at LenelS2. It allows users to send a temporary “guest pass” that visiting guests can access right from their smart phone.

Users set an activation and a deletion date/time along with any specific doors they want their guest to have access to. With this feature, users don’t need to create credentials and badges normally reserved for employees nor do they have to keep someone on stand by to let guests in.

Since its introduction to Elements, Guest Pass has been a major differentiator to competition and has been included in every demo to potential customers. Additionally, Guest Pass has received numerous requests for additional features and functionality to make it even more useful.

caseStudy.title: Who is LenelS2?
paragraphs: LenelS2 is a global leader in advanced physical security solutions, including access control, video surveillance and mobile credentialing. They’re famous for products such as OnGuard, Elements, and BlueDiamond.

caseStudy.title: What is Elements?
paragraphs: Elements is an SaaS all-in-one cloud-based access control and video management solution that helps companies monitor and secure their facilities with equipment such as cameras and badge readers.

caseStudy.title: Problem
paragraphs: Before Guest Pass, whenever a customer wanted to give access to a temporary guest in Elements (e.g., job candidate, clients, etc.), they had to add them to their Elements system and remember to remove them once access needed to be revoked.
Customers needed a way to temporarily grant access to temporary guests without adding them as a person in the system—something typically reserved for more long-term visitors such as employees.

caseStudy.title: Solution
paragraphs: Create a feature that allows users to create a temporary “guest pass” that will automatically activate and delete according to user specifications.

caseStudy.title: Users
bullets: A person who works at a company and is inviting a guest to their company’s facility (e.g., a hiring manager inviting someone for an interview, or management inviting a business client for a meeting)
A guest who was invited to the company’s facility

subsections.title: Issuing a Guest Pass
bullets: Users need to be able to add guest passes and send them themselves as they are responsible for the guest
Optional start/end date
Assign door readers to the guest pass
A method to share the guest pass
An email template for when a guest pass is shared
The ability to update a guest pass

subsections.title: Using a Guest Pass
bullets: Guest can receive their guest pass via email with link to web UI
UI shows when the pass is valid for and the customer (who will be issuing the guest pass)
Buttons available for each reader to unlock

subsections.title: Events
bullets: Events in the Elements event feed shows when a guest pass is used and where it was used

caseStudy.title: Flow & Design
paragraphs: I created a user flow using low fidelity wireframes in InVision Freehand, then moved into high fidelity designs in Figma using the pre-established LenelS2 design system that I helped manage and expand.
bullets: User navigates to the “Guest Passes” area of Elements
User selects the add button to add a guest pass
User inputs a name, activation and expiration dates and times, and the recipient email
User adds readers
User generates the pass

In high fidelity, I replaced the email recipient field with a share button that opens a separate view for recipients—preventing automatic resharing on edits and enabling users to copy/share the link via any communication method

caseStudy.title: Guest-Facing UI & Animation
paragraphs: Once the creation process was designed, I moved onto the web UI that guest pass recipients would use.

Because guest passes live on isolated pages outside the primary Elements interface, I was able to add animation to the unlock buttons to help guests understand that something is happening after they tap to unlock a door reader.
You can find a prototype of these animations [here](https://cloud.protopie.io/p/9964619755).
Additionally, I created a rough translation of the prototype in code using CodePen that you can find [here](https://codepen.io/JEScarani/pen/oNWKmxG).

caseStudy.title: Conclusion & Results
paragraphs: Guest Pass was an enjoyable problem to tackle because it serves two types of users: a customer who owns an Elements-based access control system, and a guest who is visiting one of the customer’s locations.
I learned a lot about defining user flows in a complex system and about using animation to make designs feel more responsive.
Guest Pass was successfully put into production in Q4 of 2021. Since implementation it has become a major differentiator, with users requesting additional features and functionality.

constraints: Needed to fit within an existing product ecosystem and patterns
Had to support two user groups: pass issuers and visiting guests
process: Defined users, roles, permissions, and key workflows (issuer + guest)
Mapped end-to-end flow (create pass → share → use → manage/expire)
Created low-fi flow and wireframes (InVision Freehand)
Produced hi-fi designs in Figma using the LenelS2 design system
Designed guest-facing web UI and button animations for clearer feedback
impact: Became a major differentiator and included in every customer demo
Drove follow-up requests for additional features and functionality

projects.title: JRCS Command Task

heroImage.alt: JRCS Command Task project cover image

projects.problem: JRCS provided two existing screens for a shipboard command application and asked me to design a collision warning method that grabs attention, provides actionable information, and doesn’t drown out other critical data.

caseStudy.title: The Problem
paragraphs: One of the JRCS Digital Innovation Lab’s flagship products is a command application that provides captains and officers on ships at sea with information regarding the navigation of their vessel, and highlights nearby vessels through AR as well.
The team provided me two screens and asked me to design a method for the application to warn users when they are about to collide with another vessel.

caseStudy.title: Users and Requirements
paragraphs: I started by recognizing the primary users and establishing requirements for an effective warning experience.

subsections.title: Users
bullets: Captains
Officers on “watch”

subsections.title: Requirements
bullets: Must grab users’ attention
Must provide enough information for users to act upon
Cannot drown out any other important information

caseStudy.title: Sketches
paragraphs: After establishing users and requirements, I sketched out several ideas. My immediate thought was a popover that points to the vessel the user is about to collide with.
I experimented with three different shapes and ultimately settled on a simple rectangular popover window that provided users with the needed information.

I also considered adding lines to indicate the path each ship was going to take. After moving on from sketches, I scrapped that idea because it could be difficult to implement in rough sea conditions and would add significant computational complexity.
Because the application also includes a compass-like view, I explored a way to provide warning in that view.

caseStudy.title: Lo-fi Wireframes
paragraphs: After sketches, I focused on the primary view and created a couple low-fi variations for the popover.

caseStudy.title: Hi-fi Wireframes and Progression
paragraphs: After deciding on a structure, I stylized the popover using the existing UI style so it matched the application’s design standards.
From there, I progressively refined the design by adding clearer labels and a boat indicator.

caseStudy.title: Pulse (Attention Mechanism)
paragraphs: Inspired by FPS games like Halo and Call of Duty, I added a pulsing red bar at the top of the screen to draw attention without being overwhelming.

caseStudy.title: Accessibility checks
paragraphs: I validated legibility across background conditions (sunrise/sunset/night) and ran the design through color-blind filters to ensure the warning remained accessible.

caseStudy.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: While the team liked my designs, they could only hire one person and I ultimately did not get the role. It was still a fun problem to design for, and I’m proud of the work.

constraints: Time-boxed scope with limited domain context
Must grab attention without drowning out critical navigation information
Needed to communicate decisions and trade-offs clearly
process: Clarified goals, users, and requirements from the prompt
Sketched warning concepts and evaluated feasibility trade-offs
Created lo-fi wireframes and compared layout options
Developed hi-fi designs aligned to existing UI standards
Validated legibility across backgrounds and checked accessibility with color-blind filters
impact: Delivered a structured warning concept with clear rationale and trade-offs
Demonstrated prioritization under time constraints and attention to accessibility

projects.title: Vendor-Client Gateway Redesign

heroImage.alt: Vendor-Client Gateway Redesign project cover image

projects.problem: The Vendor Client Gateway is an add-on module for CobbleStone Software’s contract management platform, Contract Insights. As part of a broader UX overhaul across the platform, I led the redesign to improve usability, modernize the experience, and increase adoption of the module.

caseStudy.title: Overview
paragraphs: The Vendor Client Gateway is an add-on module for CobbleStone Software’s contract management platform, Contract Insights.
This project focused on redesigning a historically underperforming part of the product—one that had become a consistent source of frustration for both clients and internal teams.
As part of a broader UX overhaul across the platform, I led the redesign of the gateway to improve usability, modernize the experience, and increase adoption of the module.

caseStudy.title: Problem
paragraphs: The existing gateway experience created significant friction for users and limited the product’s effectiveness.
bullets: Non-responsive design, making the experience difficult on mobile devices
Outdated UI patterns that reduced usability and trust
Over-customization, allowing clients to configure the interface in ways that harmed usability
Inconsistent workflows, particularly around solicitations management

caseStudy.title: Approach
bullets: Audit & evaluation of the existing gateway
Competitive inspiration from similar vendor portals
Design simplification to reduce harmful customization
Iterative design with internal feedback

caseStudy.title: Solution
bullets: Responsive layouts across desktop and mobile
Improved navigation and information hierarchy
Standardized UI patterns
Simplified configurations to reduce UX risk

caseStudy.title: Future Plans
paragraphs: Phase 1 of a 3-phase roadmap: redesign existing features (current), expand capabilities, and iterate based on feedback.

constraints: Non-responsive design made the experience difficult on mobile devices
Outdated UI patterns reduced usability and trust
Over-customization allowed configurations that harmed usability
Inconsistent workflows, especially around solicitations management
Balanced user needs, business goals, and technical constraints in a mature enterprise product
process: Audit & evaluation: reviewed the existing gateway to identify usability issues, inconsistencies, and outdated patterns
Competitive inspiration: analyzed similar vendor portals to understand modern expectations and interaction models
Design simplification: reduced excessive customization in favor of structured, scalable patterns
Iterative design: developed updated flows, wireframes, and high-fidelity designs and refined them with internal feedback
impact: Expected: reduced friction in key workflows
Expected: increased adoption and usage of the gateway module
